Looking at the function, the key logic is on line 73: `if now > effective: return None`

The condition checks if `now > effective` (strictly greater than). This means an entry is considered valid when `now == effective` (i.e., exactly at expiry time). However, the typical expectation for TTL is that an entry has expired when `now >= effective` (the entry should not be returned when it's exactly at expiry).

The boundary condition is off-by-one in terms of the comparison operator. The entry should be expired when `now >= effective`, not just when `now > effective`.
